advantages: Quantify uncertainty around a point estimate with a single interpretable range instead of implying false precision.
Connect directly to hypothesis tests: exclusion of a null value from a (1 − α) CI matches rejection at significance α for standard two-sided tests.
Scale naturally with sample size—larger n narrows the interval, rewarding more data collection.
Well-established formulas and libraries exist for means, proportions, and regression coefficients; bootstrap extends the pattern to custom metrics.
disadvantages: Frequentist interpretation is easy to misstate; many stakeholders hear '95% chance' when the correct statement is about procedure coverage.
Classic Wald intervals for proportions can be too narrow or even outside [0, 1] for small n or extreme p̂; better methods require extra care.
Intervals assume a sampling model (often simple random sampling); violated assumptions (clustering, peeking, non-i.i.d. data) break nominal coverage.
A CI does not by itself answer causal or business questions—it bounds a parameter under a model, not whether a change is worth shipping.
